title: Division and Distribution of Distinct Items

concepts.title: Difference between Divison and Distribution
concepts.conceptBody:
Division and distribution

Before going to formula and questions on division and distribution, let us understand the difference between the two.

Let us take an example to get hold of the point

(where $F_1$, $F_2$ & $F_3$ are three group & $E_1$, $E_2$ & $E_3$ are items put in these groups)

$\therefore$ We can say that, in division, the order of selection (division) does not matter.

Generally, we say

Division among group

Distribution among people

because group's order does not matter where as order of people do matter as they cannot be identical.

You can say that division $\equiv$ selection

distribution $\equiv$ arrangement

So, we have divided division & distribution into 3 cases and we will now do them in detail individually.

concepts.title: Divison and Distribution into groups of Unequal Sizes
concepts.conceptBody:
Groups of Unequal Size 

Number of ways to divide $\left( m+n \right)$ items into 2 unequal groups containing $m\And n$ items respectively is

$\frac{\left( \text{m}+\text{n} \right)!}{\text{m}!\text{n}!}$

Logic: It is simple. Just select $m$ elements first from $\left( m+n \right)$ things & then $n$ out of $n$ remaining

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{m+n}}{{C}_{m}}\times {{~}^{n}}{{C}_{n}}={{~}^{m+n}}{{C}_{m}}=\frac{\left( m+n \right)!}{m!n!}$

we will call this as basic result

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Basic result $=\frac{\left( m+n \right)!}{m!n!}$

Now, distribution

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The number of ways to distribute $\left( \text{m}+\text{n} \right)$ items into 3 persons in the groups containing $\text{m}$ $\And \text{n}$ items respectively is

$=$ (no. of ways to divide) (no. of groups)

$=$ Basic result (no. of groups)!

$=\frac{\left( m+n \right)!}{m!n!}\times 2!$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ For case 1: 

Q) (a) Divide 6 balls in 3 boxes of different sizes such that one box contains 1 ball, another contains 2 and remaining contains 3. 

(b) Divide 6 balls in 3 boxes of different sizes such that box 1 contain 1 ball, box 2 contains 2 balls & box 3 contains 3 balls. 

(c) Divide 6 balls in 3 identical boxes where one box contains 1 ball, another contains 2 balls & rest contains 3. 

Solution: 

This is to illustrate you, how to figure out the difference between division and distribution, and then apply the formula.

First let us find out the basic result

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Basic Result $=\frac{6!}{1!2!3!}=60$

Now we will see the difference,

(a) 6 balls in 3 boxes, no other constraint except that 1 box contains 1, other 2 & remaining 3.

Let ${{G}_{1}}$ be group of balls containing 1 ball

6${{\text{G}}_{2}}$ be group of balls containing 2 balls

${{\text{G}}_{3}}$ be group of balls containing 3 balls

 

Now here there will be more cases where we can shift groups also i.e.

and so, on

But this is not division, in fact this is distribution now using formula

for distribution = Basic Result (no. of groups)!

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=60\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3! \\ {} & ~=360 \\ \end{array}$

(b) Now here it is fixed that box 1, contains 1 ball

Box 2 contains $2\And $ Box 3 contains 3 balls

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ This means that this is only division, no distribution possible

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total ways $=$ Basic Result

$=60$

(c) Now it is given that boxes are identical i.e. all boxes are same & they cannot be differentiated. No such box 1, box 2, box3 now.

So, it becomes a case of division only.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total ways $=$ Basic Result

$=60$

concepts.title: Divison and Distribution into groups of Equal Sizes
concepts.conceptBody:
Groups of Equal Sizes 

This case is the division/distribution of items into different or number or groups, where groups contain equal no. of items.

Let us understand the concept with the following question.

Q) Divide/distribute 

(a) 9 different balls in 3 different boxes, equally. 

(b) 9 different balls in 3 identical boxes, equally. 

(c) divide 52 cards equally in 4 parts 

(d) 52 cards equally among 4 players. 

Solution: 

(a) 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total no. of ways (using the formula) $=$ Basic Result

$=\frac{9!}{3!3!3!}$

(b) Now here boxes are identical

$\therefore $ There is no distribution between Box 1, Box $2 ~\And$ Box 3. So, the shift which was valid in case (a) above is not valued here as there is no Box 1, Box 2 or Box 3.

$\Rightarrow$ It’s a case of division only.

$\therefore$ Total ways $=\frac{\text{ Basic }\text{ Result }}{\left( \text{ no}\text{. }\text{ of }\text{ groups } \right)!}$

(c) Welcome to cards question (I)

(This is analogous to game of sweep)

The question says dividing in 4 parts. Here parts are indistinguishable or we can say parts are identical.

$\therefore$ Clearly, it’s a case of division only.

Total ways $=\frac{52!}{\left( 13!13!13!13! \right)}\times \frac{1}{4!}=\frac{52!}{{{(13!)}^{4}}\times 4!}$

(d) Now we have to divide in 4 players

Now here these cases matter

Hence it is case of distribution

$\therefore$ Total ways $=$ Basic Result

$=\frac{52!}{{{(13!)}^{4}}}$

concepts.title: Divison and Distribution into groups of both Equal and Unequal Sizes
concepts.conceptBody:
Groups of Both Equal & Unequal Sizes 

In this case we find out the total ways in which we can divide/distribute items into groups containing equal as well as unequal groups. For ex.

This is an example of case 3 as we have 2 kinds of groups here.

I ${{}^{\text{st }}}$ group has 3 elements &

${{2}^{\text{nd }}}$ group has 2 elements

$\And $ hence both have unequal sizes.

Here also there will be case of division and distribution but distribution is divided in 2 parts.

 

Q) Five balls of different colors are to be placed in 3 boxes of different sizes. Each box can hold all five balls. In how many different ways can we place the balls so that no box remains empty. 

Solution: Since there is no distinction between boxes this becomes a case of distribution as we have seen in previous cases also.

There are only two cases possible.

Hence total ways $=90+60$

$=150$

Q) 7 departments are to be divided among 3 ministers such that every minister should get atleast $1\And $ atmost 4 departments. In how many ways can this be done? 

Solution: 

This is a very practical problem which we encounter at the time of elections. Though it is clear from the question only that this is a case of distribution only, as any minister can have any number of departments.

Making the table to see the no. of possible cases

We cannot make more cases as there is a maximum limit of 4 departments per minister.

Also note that here case 1 falls into the category of (case-I) i.e. distribution among unequal groups, so you have to be careful while deciding cases.

$\begin{array}{l} \therefore \text{ Total }\text{ ways } & =630+420+630 \\ {} & =1680 \\ \end{array}$

We will consider an example of DAEG case later on.

examples.title: Eleven books consisting of 5 Mathematics, 4 Physics and 2 Chemistry are placed on a shelf. The number of possible ways of arranging them on the assumption that the books of the same subject are all together is
The number of groups that can be made from 5 different green balls, 4 different blue balls and 3 different red balls, if at least 1 green and 1 blue ball is to be included
In how many ways can 5 red and 4 white balls be drawn from a bag containing 10 red and 8 white balls
Out of 5 apples, 10 mangoes and 15 oranges, any 15 fruits distributed among two persons. The total number of ways of distribution
